If the device uses the original protocol, it will not respond to the default message at all. The function _recieve_msg will timeout instead. So there is no way to differentiate between a broken device and a device that uses the original protocol based on the first query_status message bytearray([0x81, 0x8a, 0x8b]). 

Only when you fall back to the original protocol will the device send a response. And once you fall back, you may as well specifiy the expected length when it is different. 


This is mostly an optimization issue, isn't it? As in, we need 2 retries with the default protocol before we fall back to the original protocol. Is there a faster way to do this? cycle through the protocols before doing the 1st and 2nd retry of the other protocols perhaps?

Yes, I think we could refactor this into a function, but I don't think that's strictly necessary because the three tests are the only times these modules will be reloaded. Let me know what you want me to do with this.

Reloading the modules is necessary to pick up the overridden settings, because simply overriding the settings doesn't re-run the

    WATCHMAN_LOGIN = getattr(settings, 'WATCHMAN_LOGIN', False)

logic in `settings.py`, or the

    if settings.WATCHMAN_LOGIN:
        ....

logic in `decorators.py`, or the

    from watchman.decorators import token_required, login_required

logic in `views.py`, but re-importing all of those modules in that specific order does.

The [`@override_settings` decorator](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.4/topics/testing/#django.test.utils.override_settings) automatically ensures that the settings do not propagate outside of the decorated test/s (it can also decorate classes).

Couldn't we check inside the new folder for test files? This is what I meant by getting rid of folder checking and just checking the flles.
Where `$ git status --short` will return:
```
 M project/accounts/tests/test_api.py
?? newapp/
```

$ git status --short --untracked-files' will return:
```
 M project/accounts/tests/test_api.py
?? newapp/__init__.py
?? newapp/admin.py
?? newapp/apps.py
?? newapp/migrations/__init__.py
?? newapp/models.py
?? newapp/tests/test_views.py
?? newapp/views.py
```
So that we can just check by the file names. see: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-status/1.8.1#git-status---untracked-filesltmodegt

Oddly enough, whenever an untracked folder is added, git status immediately shows the individual files.

The S3 storage backend doesn't actually return the deleted item marker or state and the delete operation is asynchronous. The deletion state of the file can not be checked with the current S3BotoStorage storage API. This is the original reason for writing deletion here this way. The old implementation checks if the file has been deleted, but that always raises because the S3 bucket is not in sync yet.

This is due to limitations in the S3BotoStorage interface. I am going to ask for a refactor in that package as well, but that will be a breaking API change, whereas this one should not be.

Possible ways to implement checking if deletion works are:

- Wait for a small while before checking, trying delays between e.g. in 300-3000ms range. This could return false positives due to async calls;
- Implement locks and only allow one check to run here per time. Check if there is more than one file to be deleted. This should work but limits the health checker rate severely.
- Implement the deletion state checker independently of the S3BotoStorage API, fetch delete markers and compare them. This is an extremely volatile approach which is tied to the Boto API version.

So, really, there is no clean way for doing this yet.

But I wonder - if you could rely on Python 3.5, would the implementation have been different, or would you have had to override all of this behavior not to ignore hidden files? For example, if you look at the recent `setuptools.sdist.py36compat.py`, you'll see functionality that's a backport of code in Python 3.7, which can be removed when Python 3.7 is the minimum supported version. So, I'm actually not a fan of a decorator here.  This is a check which needs to happen, but it also looks like something which was sprinkled around without a ton of consideration as to when we're raising exceptions and how. It's called in places where it's detrimental (`stop`) and called places where it'll be ineffective (`_prepare_request` and `_send_request`). For the former case, it should be called after `self._running = False`, but you can't do that because it's a decorator.  In that latter case, it'll crash a background thread by raising an exception which won't be raised to the user and might kill an otherwise healthy thread.

This is why I don't like to decorator approach. It encourages adding it all over the place "just to be safe" because decorators look safe. Maybe it's just how I perceive it, but if this were a function call at the top of user-facing functions (i.e. a Guard), it'd be less likely to be added as it has been.

Maybe that's just me, though. Keep the decorator if you think it's useful, but right now it's used in way too many places. It should be used to guard user-facing functions, and in cases where it's not going to hurt things which otherwise should be happening (like shutting down the producer).

Interesting. So I think AppVeyor must be considering a command to have failed if it writes to stderr, regardless of the exit code. Perhaps even if the tests pass, they sometimes write directly to stderr. Our existing tooling only cares about the exit code of the command. If my hypothesis is correct, there are a couple options:

- You could try redirecting stderr to a file, which would hopefully prevent the failure but you would need to make the file a build artifact for debugging. This is the simpler option but probably not the "right" solution, although certainly fine for now.
- You could try eliminating stderr when there isn't a problem. That means leaving LogItem::error in the LoggingException class but figuring out how it is being called during the build.

Python 2 and Python 3 handle warnings differently. Python 3 doesn't consider duplicates of the same warning when catching and counting.

In this test's case, the DeprecationWarning is raised 3 times, but caught only once in Python 3.

In Python 2, a warning is counted each time it is caught. This means this line will raise `AssertionError: 3 != 1`, as 3 separate warnings are raised.

This *is* using that standard location for proxies as closely as we can. The reason that normal tools don't pick up our proxies is because we encode some information about them in the file name so for example an LHCb proxy will be called `/tmp/x509up_u34874:lhcb` while a GridPP proxy will be `/tmp/x509up_u34874:gridpp`. I have deliberately named these as closely as possible as the usual names to make them easy to find. There is no risk of collision between users as every proxy is named after the user who created it. For example, log onto any `lxplus` node and do `ls -hlt /tmp/x509up_u*`:
```
-rw-------. 1 anmehta  zh     7.3K Sep 29 14:04 /tmp/x509up_u44856
-rw-------. 1 rwatari  zp     9.0K Sep 29 09:50 /tmp/x509up_u77518
-rw-------. 1 tkunigo  zp     9.0K Sep 29 05:54 /tmp/x509up_u49252
-rw-------. 1 spigazzi zh     7.7K Sep 28 17:40 /tmp/x509up_u68758
-rw-------. 1 kmistry  zp     9.1K Sep 28 17:32 /tmp/x509up_u55942
...
```

Ok, I was trying to get some opinions on it. The motivation for going in this direction was because it seems you have to consistently pass in Metric and Resource into every API call.  Seems if the common use case was to write points for a metric over time, and if those keep taking the same arguments every call, it would be better to have something to encapsulate those arguments. 

I agree it's a bit inconsistent, but it reflects the TimeSeries being slightly inconsistent at an API level- it's one of the few API calls where you call `create` multiple times to update it (most API objects are created once with a unique ID and after that only updated). TimeSeries should really have a write_point method of some sort, but it doesn't, so to do multiple writes to the same TimeSeries we can either copy the TimeSeries to a new object but replace the points (similar to what the `header` method does now but will need to add keyword argument magic), or keep recreating similar objects with the same arguments. Both seemed clumsy to me so I was putting an alternative out there but sounds like you're not a fan.
